content.data: 优化一下 /workspaces/devcontainer_latex/tsx-slides/ipdps2026_fasttt_slides.tex 这份幻灯片，我需要在学术会议上做 oral presentation ，我的论文原文在 /workspaces/devcontainer_latex/tsx-overleaf 文件夹下，IPDPS 会议官方给出的 oral pre 指导是 /workspaces/devcontainer_latex/tsx-slides/guide.md 。我不想让幻灯片每页的文字太多，尽量展示出论文原文中的所有图片，优化图片排版，你可以自由优化幻灯片。讲稿也连带优化，在/workspaces/devcontainer_latex/tsx-slides/ipdps2026_fasttt_slides.tex中的note块中是每页的讲稿。

data.text: I'm starting by looking at the directory structure to see what we're working with and get a sense of how the slides and paper are currently organized.

# Outline

Motivation (1 slide)– clearly, articulate the motivation behind the specific problem you are solving and the significance if the approach is successful.

Limitations of existing solutions (1-2 slides) — clearly, articulate the most relevant prior solutions in your problem domain and how your approach relates to or differentiates from them.

Approach and key ideas (5-6 slides) — two or three most novel ideas/findings of your approach. A few things that everyone should remember after the presentation. It is okay if the ideas are not described with 100% detail in the slides or during the talk.

Flagship results (1-3 slides) — your flagship experimental/theoretical results. Limit to one or two key results. Be brief but impressive. Even if the paper has results with five competing approaches, consider how best to convey impact by highlighting a subset of all the data in the paper. 

Ongoing/future research & Summary (1 slide) — Summarize your paper in a sentence or two. Explicitly list open or unresolved research questions and new research direction to facilitate discussion.

# Other Requirements

Do not try to include all the methodological details or results – try to highlight a few important ones. Do not to discuss all the design and implementation details – try to highlight only the most novel and important ones. Avoid repeated information.

Remember, the key is to get other people interested to read your paper after the presentation, and to help them remember a few key ideas from your approach.

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:introduction}

Ensuring high data availability in the presence of hardware or software failures is a fundamental function for modern large-scale distributed storage systems~\cite{fordAvailabilityGloballyDistributed2010} and consensus in distributed networks~\cite{ecForConsensusIpdps2022}. 
Two mainstream approaches to fault tolerance are \emph{replication} and \emph{erasure coding}. 
Replication maintains multiple identical copies of data, offering simplicity but incurring substantial storage overhead. 
In contrast, erasure coding partitions data into $k$ data chunks and generates $m$ parity chunks (generally smaller than $k$) through mathematical encoding, enabling the same or higher reliability with significantly lower storage overhead~\cite{weatherspoonErasureCodingVs2002}. 
This efficiency has led to its adoption in production-scale systems such as the Google File System (GFS)~\cite{ghemawatGoogleFileSystem2003}, Windows Azure Storage~\cite{calderWindowsAzureStorage2011, huangErasureCodingWindows2012}, Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)~\cite{shvachkoHadoopDistributedFile2010, chiniahDynamicErasureCoding2019}, and Ceph~\cite{weilCephScalableHighPerformance2006, weilCephReliableScalable2007}.

Classical Reed–Solomon (RS) codes~\cite{reedPolynomialCodesCertain1960} are the most widely used erasure codes, providing the Maximum Distance Separable (MDS) property over finite fields. However, their encoding and decoding require computationally expensive finite-field multiplications.
%often implemented over $GF(2^w)$ with $w>1$. 
To improve performance, some operations can be transformed into XOR computations~\cite{lubyXORBasedErasureResilientCoding1995}
%over $GF(2)$~\cite{lubyXORBasedErasureResilientCoding1995}
, and numerous system-level optimizations have been proposed to accelerate RS codes, including matrix structure refinement\cite{zhouFastErasureCoding2020}, common subexpression reuse\cite{uezatoAcceleratingXORbasedErasure2021}, memory and cache access optimizations\cite{uezatoAcceleratingXORbasedErasure2021,wangFastAccelerationStrategies2025}, pointer overhead reduction\cite{wangFastAccelerationStrategies2025}, and workload balance~\cite{repairIpdps2024}. While these optimizations have been integrated into high-performance libraries such as Jerasure~\cite{plankJerasureLibraryFacilitating2008, arslanOpenMPPOSIXThreads2017}, G-CRS~\cite{liuGCRSGPUAccelerated2018}, Zerasure~\cite{zhouFastErasureCoding2020}, SLPEC~\cite{uezatoAcceleratingXORbasedErasure2021}, Cerasure~\cite{wangFastAccelerationStrategies2025}, and Intel ISA-L~\cite{gajalwarExploringDataProtection2024}, even state-of-the-art RS implementations may struggle to fully exploit the bandwidth of emerging high-speed storage and networking hardware.

Besides the classical RS codes, a distinct line of studies have focused on non-cyclic \emph{\sxor} erasure codes, which avoid the heavy finite-field matrix multiplications. \emph{\SXOR} erasure codes allow encoding and decoding using bit-shift and exclusive-or (XOR) operations, thus featuring low computational costs. ZigZag Decodable (ZD) codes~\cite{gongZigzagDecodableCodes2017} demonstrated the feasibility of constructing \sxor codes from Vandermonde or Hankel structures. Subsequent research on ZigZag codes has primarily focused on reducing the required decoding space~\cite{fuOverheadFreeInPlaceRecovery2014} and enhancing storage efficiency~\cite{fuOverheadFreeInPlaceRecovery2015,fuDecodingRepairSchemes2020}. Recently, \twotone \sxor Codes~\cite{fuTwotoneShiftXORStorage2021} were introduced to generalize the construction and decoding schemes. This class of codes has the \emph{successive solvability} property~\cite{chengSuccessivelySolvableShiftAdd2021}, allowing decoding to proceed in a ordered manner which can be predetermined before the decoding. 
%This structured design improves memory access regularity and offers greater potential for system-level optimization.

While \twotone \sxor codes offer compelling theoretical advantages, their practical implementation for modern computing systems remains unexplored. Through a systematic analysis of the intuitive \twotone implementation, we identify three key performance bottlenecks.
First, in both the encoding and decoding processes, the intuitive implementation processes data chunks sequentially, performing one parity chunk update per scan. While this approach maintains good spatial locality for data chunk access, it results in redundant memory accesses (reading/writing parity chunks multiple times) and poor temporal locality for parity chunk operations.
Second, the encoding process can employ either horizontal or vertical traversal patterns for accessing data chunks. However, both exhibit critical limitations: Horizontal traversal causes repeated parity chunk writes, while vertical traverse requires redundant data chunk reloads. Neither pattern achieves optimal memory efficiency. 
Third, prior work often overlooks the equal importance of parity chunks in maintaining system reliability. When both data and parity chunks are lost simultaneously, our analysis reveals that a joint recovery approach offers substantial performance advantages over the conventional separate handling method. By processing data and parity recoveries together, we eliminate redundant operations. In addition, for different data erasure cases, applying the same chunk traversal strategy may lead to suboptimal performance.

To bridge the gap between the theoretically efficient yet practically underperforming implementations of \twotone \sxor erasure codes, we present \design, the first high-performance implementation optimized for modern storage systems. To our knowledge, \design represents the first comprehensive optimization framework specifically designed for the state-of-the-art \twotone \sxor erasure codes. By revisiting the original encoding and decoding processes of \twotone, we identify several critical performance bottlenecks, which motivates the design of \design.
The main contributions of \design are summarized as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item To mitigate redundant memory traffic in both encoding and decoding processes of the original \twotone implementation, we introduce a Cache-Friendly Window Partitioning technique that optimizes data access patterns for reduced memory bandwidth consumption and improved cache utilization.
    \item To further minimize memory accesses to parity chunks during encoding, we propose Neighbor-Merging Access, a technique that accumulates intermediate results in CPU registers before writing to parity buffers. This approach reduces memory traffic and enhances overall encoding performance.
    \item We design a unified Erasure-Parity Mapping abstraction that consolidates all erasure cases into a single and generalized decoding path, which eliminates redundant copying and repair stages and enables efficient multi-erasure recovery. Additionally, we integrate a novel Traversal Pattern Selection scheme to accelerate the most frequent single-erasure decoding case with an optimized fast path.
    \item We implement \design as a high-performance plugin compatible with modern storage systems such as Ceph, enabling seamless integration and direct comparison with other erasure coding implementations. Across diverse (k,m) configurations, \design demonstrates consistent throughput gains of over 30\% on average and up to 182.1\% compared to state-of-the-art libraries, including ISA-L \cite{isa-l} and Cerasure \cite{wangFastAccelerationStrategies2025}. Furthermore, end-to-end file storage benchmarks in Ceph show that \design outperforms the native ISA-L implementation by up to 13.3\%.
\end{itemize}

% Our main contributions are as follows:
% \begin{itemize}
%     \item \textbf{Generalized decoding framework:} We introduce an \emph{erasure–parity mapping} abstraction that unifies all data and parity loss patterns into a single decoding path, eliminating redundant conditional logic and enabling simultaneous data recovery and parity regeneration.
%     \item \textbf{Memory and cache optimizations:} We propose a \emph{cache-friendly window} mechanism with on-demand initialization to improve locality, and a \emph{neighbor-merging access} pattern that reduces memory accesses by combining adjacent data chunks in registers before writing to parity buffers.
%     \item \textbf{Special-case acceleration:} For the common single-erasure scenario, we implement a specialized fast path leveraging SIMD streaming stores to bypass caches and minimize memory pollution.
%     \item \textbf{Production-grade integration and evaluation:} We integrate \design into Ceph’s erasure coding framework and conduct extensive experiments across multiple $(k,m)$ configurations, comparing against ISA-L, Jerasure, and Cerasure. Results show that \design achieves higher encoding throughput and significantly faster recovery under diverse failure patterns.
% \end{itemize}

%By bridging the gap between theoretical algorithm design and engineering practice, this work not only delivers concrete performance improvements but also establishes a reusable optimization methodology for shift–XOR erasure codes in large-scale distributed storage systems.

\begin{abstract}
Erasure coding is a widely used redundancy technique for fault tolerance in data storage systems, offering significant space savings. 
However, it generally incurs substantial computational overhead. 
While existing research has explored various optimization techniques for classical Reed-Solomon (RS) erasure codes, more recent \sxor codes, which feature significantly lower computational complexity, have received comparatively little attention. 
In this paper, we focus on \twotone, the state-of-the-art \sxor code, and present the first comprehensive and high-optimized implementation of \twotone, \design. 
Due to its inherent shifting pattern, \twotone encounters challenges including redundant memory access operations and inefficient data traversal patterns. 
To address these issues, we propose a suite of optimization techniques: 
(1) Cache-Friendly Window Partitioning to improve data locality in both encoding and decoding; 
(2) Neighbor-Merging Access that combines adjacent data operations within registers to reduce memory traffic in encoding; 
(3) Erasure–Parity Mapping that simplifies the decoding process of all data and parity erased cases; 
and (4) Traversal Pattern Selection for accelerating the single-erasure recovery by exploiting the vectorization store operations.
Validated across multiple configurations, \design consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art erasure coding libraries, including ISA-L and Cerasure, achieving an average encoding throughput improvement of 36.2\% (up to 84.1\%) and an average decoding throughput improvement of 33.5\% (up to 182.1\%).
\end{abstract}
% (i) a unified erasure–parity mapping framework that supports efficient decoding for arbitrary combinations of data and parity losses through a single decoding path; (ii) a cache-friendly window partitioning mechanism with on-demand initialization; (iii) a neighbor-merging access pattern that minimizes memory accesses by coalescing adjacent data operations within registers; and (iv) a specialized fast path for single-erasure recovery that leverages SIMD streaming stores for accelerated execution.

%Erasure coding, which is a widely adopted technique for achieving high reliability in large-scale distributed storage systems with significantly lower storage overhead than replication. Among various schemes, \twotone non-cyclic \sxor erasure codes offer attractive theoretical performance by replacing finite-field multiplications with simple shift and XOR operations, enabling linear-time encoding and decoding. 
%However, their practical deployment has been hindered by the lack of generalized decoding support, poor cache locality, excessive memory traffic, and the absence of production-grade integration.

%This paper presents a system-level optimized implementation of \twotone non-cyclic shift--XOR erasure codes, integrated as a pluggable erasure coding backend in the Ceph storage platform. We introduce four complementary optimizations: (\emph{i}) a unified erasure--parity mapping framework that handles arbitrary data and parity loss patterns in a single decoding path; (\emph{ii}) a cache-friendly window partitioning mechanism with on-demand initialization; (\emph{iii}) a neighbor-merging access pattern that reduces memory accesses by combining adjacent data operations in registers; and (\emph{iv}) a specialized fast path for single-erasure recovery leveraging SIMD streaming stores.

%Extensive evaluations across multiple $(k,m)$ configurations demonstrate that our implementation achieves up to $150\%$ higher encoding throughput and substantially faster recovery compared to state-of-the-art RS-based codes such as Intel ISA-L, while also outperforming existing \sxor variants like ZigZag. By bridging the gap between algorithmic theory and engineering practice, this work establishes a practical optimization methodology for \sxor erasure codes, enabling their efficient adoption in production-scale distributed storage systems.

\section{Performance Evaluation}

\subsection{Experimental Setup}
In this section, we evaluate the performance of our proposed \design across multiple configurations, compare it with state-of-the-art baseline solutions, and analyze the individual contributions of each optimization technique.
\paragraph{Environment}
All experiments were conducted on a high-performance workstation equipped with an Intel Core i9-14900K (6.0 GHz, 24 cores, 36 MiB L3) and 64 GB DDR5 memory. The system runs Ubuntu 22.04 with GCC 11.4.0, and AVX2 instructions are enabled.

\paragraph{Baselines}
We compare our \design against three representative erasure coding baselines. 1) Jerasure \cite{plankJerasureLibraryFacilitating2008}, a widely adopted erasure coding library in storage systems \cite{ceph,khanRethinkingErasureCodes2012} and the official solution supported by Ceph; 2) ISA-L \cite{isa-l}, an open-source, hardware-accelerated software library highly optimized by Intel, which is also officially supported by Ceph and widely regarded as the most performant production-grade implementation; 3) Cerasure \cite{wangFastAccelerationStrategies2025}, a state-of-the-art academic erasure coding implementation optimized for high efficiency.

\paragraph{Implementations}
Our proposed \design is implemented in C++ to ensure high performance, with the codebase comprising approximately 2000 lines of code. Notably, Jerasure and ISA-L are officially supported and integrated into Ceph, enabling direct benchmarking within its ecosystem.
To ensure correctness and fairness in performance measurement, we employ Ceph's built-in benchmarking utility, \texttt{ceph\_erasure\_code\_benchmark}\footnote{\url{https://github.com/ceph/ceph/src/test/erasure-code}}, for comparisons with Jerasure and ISA-L.
% As for comparison with Cerasure, since it is not officially supported by Ceph, we directly compare our \design implementation with its open-source codebase \footnote{\url{https://github.com/WeiWangvv/cerasure}} and follow its original benchmarking methodology to maintain methodological consistency.
For Cerasure (unsupported by Ceph), we benchmark \design against its open-source codebase\footnote{\url{https://github.com/WeiWangvv/cerasure}} following its original methodology to ensure consistency.
Without explicit comments, the experiments are conducted by Ceph's benchmarking utility. The used version of Ceph is v18.2.7.

\paragraph{Evaluation Configurations}
We evaluate all schemes under four representative $(k,m)$ configurations: $(6,2)$, $(8,3)$, $(10,4)$, and $(6,3)$, covering storage overhead ratios from 1.33 to 1.5. These settings are chosen to reflect both prevalent industry practice and prior research: $(6,2)$ is a canonical layout in RAID-6 deployments; $(6,3)$ has been adopted in large-scale cloud storage systems such as Google’s~\cite{fordAvailabilityGloballyDistributed2010}; $(10,4)$ is preferred in certain HDFS-RAID deployments at Facebook~\cite{sathiamoorthyXORingElephantsNovel2013}; and $(8,3)$ matches the evaluation setting used in the original \twotone study~\cite{fuTwotoneShiftXORStorage2021}. For each configuration, the chunk size (i.e., the length of each data or parity chunk) is fixed at 2\,MB. We report the average throughput of repeating 2000 times of encoding and decoding for each experiment.
%The same configurations, chunk size, and iteration counts are applied in both evaluation environments. 

\subsection{Coding Throughput Evaluation}
We first evaluate the overall encoding and decoding performance of \design and the compared baselines in terms of throughput. Note that the experimental results from Ceph’s benchmarking utility and those obtained using Cerasure’s configuration may differ due to differences in setup or implementation. Therefore, we report these results separately.

\noindent \textbf{Encoding Performance.} Table~\ref{tab:ceph-encode} reports the encoding throughput for various $(k,m)$ configurations of \design, ISA-L and Jerasure under Ceph's benchmarking utility. 
% Across all tested configurations, \design achieves substantial gains over both baselines. Compared to the best production-grade solution, ISA-L, throughput improvements range from 29.7\% to 84.1\%, while relative to Jerasure the gains range from 100.0\% to 300.4\%. 
Across all tested configurations, \design achieves substantial gains over both baselines. Compared to the best production-grade solution, ISA-L, throughput improvements range from 29.7\% to 84.1\%, with an average improvement of 59.1\%. Relative to Jerasure, the gains range from 104.0\% to 342.8\%, averaging 261.4\%.
Even in the $(10,4)$ case, where \design's own throughput drops by roughly 50\% relative to its other configurations, it still outperforms both ISA-L and Jerasure. This drop is attributable to the algorithmic structure: for the first three parity blocks, the inter-data shift offsets are 0 or 1 symbol, which can be implemented efficiently and integrated into the neighbor-merging access pattern. The fourth parity block, however, requires a shift offset of 2 symbols, which cannot be merged with the others under AVX2's 256-bit register constraints, thus reducing achievable throughput.

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption{The Encoding Throughput (GB/s) of \design, ISA-L and Jerasure}
\label{tab:ceph-encode}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Config} & \textbf{\design} & \textbf{ISA-L \cite{isa-l}} & \textbf{Jerasure \cite{plankJerasureLibraryFacilitating2008}} \\ \hline
(6, 2)          & 34.74             & 24.37        & 17.03             \\ \hline
(6, 3)          & 31.69             & 17.59        & 7.64              \\ \hline
(8, 3)          & 31.35             & 17.03        & 7.08              \\ \hline
(10, 4)         & 17.59             & 13.56        & 4.58              \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

When compared to Cerasure, the state-of-the-art Reed–Solomon (RS) erasure code implementation, our proposed \design achieves superior performance across all tested $(k,m)$ configurations. The detailed results are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:two-tone-cerasure-encode}. Specifically, \design demonstrates throughput gains ranging from 3.4\% to 27.0\% over Cerasure, with an average improvement of 13.2\%.

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption{The Encoding Throughput(GB/s) of \design and Cerasure}
\label{tab:two-tone-cerasure-encode}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Config} & \textbf{\design} & \textbf{Cerasure \cite{wangFastAccelerationStrategies2025}} \\ \hline
(6, 2)          & 47.97             & 44.20             \\ \hline
(6, 3)          & 32.01             & 30.97             \\ \hline
(8, 3)          & 32.75             & 28.72             \\ \hline
(10, 4)         & 24.58             & 19.36             \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\noindent \textbf{Decoding Performance.} Table~\ref{tab:ceph-decode} presents the decoding throughput across various $(k,m)$ configurations of \design, ISA-L and Jerasure, where the \emph{Erasures} column indicates the number of erased chunks. We highlight the \emph{Erasures} factor as critical, as \design incorporates techniques optimized for distinct erasure cases, particularly for single-chunk loss. For single-chunk erasures, \design achieves remarkable performance gains over ISA-L, with improvements exceeding 130\%. This advantage stems from a direct XOR pass that bypasses the full \ttelim procedure. Overall, \design delivers an average 50.9\% improvement over ISA-L and an average 161.1\% improvement over Jerasure. For ISA-L, the largest gains occur in single-erasure cases, with improvements from 135.4\% to 182.1\%. For Jerasure, the largest gains occur with higher erasure counts (three or four erased chunks), reaching 212.5\% to 328.8\%.

While \design generally outperforms ISA-L, some cases exhibit smaller margins or even slight regressions, most notably in two-chunk erasures and in the $(10,4)$ configuration with more than one chunk erasures.
The decoding slowdown in $(10,4)$ shares the same root cause as observed in encoding: the special 2-symbols shift offset in the fourth parity block disrupts both the \subs and \ttelim phases.
For two-erasure cases, performance is less favorable because \design cannot leverage the direct XOR path used in single-erasure recovery, nor does it benefit from the greater XOR dominance typical of higher-erasure scenarios. Instead, it must execute both \subs and \ttelim in full, leading to reduced efficiency.
Nevertheless, throughput remains close to ISA-L’s, and \design’s encoding performance is consistently higher.

\section{Performance Evaluation}

\subsection{Experimental Setup}
In this section, we evaluate the performance of our proposed \design across multiple configurations, compare it with state-of-the-art baseline solutions, and analyze the individual contributions of each optimization technique.
\paragraph{Environment}
All experiments were conducted on a high-performance workstation equipped with an Intel Core i9-14900K (6.0 GHz, 24 cores, 36 MiB L3) and 64 GB DDR5 memory. The system runs Ubuntu 22.04 with GCC 11.4.0, and AVX2 instructions are enabled.

\paragraph{Baselines}
We compare our \design against three representative erasure coding baselines. 1) Jerasure \cite{plankJerasureLibraryFacilitating2008}, a widely adopted erasure coding library in storage systems \cite{ceph,khanRethinkingErasureCodes2012} and the official solution supported by Ceph; 2) ISA-L \cite{isa-l}, an open-source, hardware-accelerated software library highly optimized by Intel, which is also officially supported by Ceph and widely regarded as the most performant production-grade implementation; 3) Cerasure \cite{wangFastAccelerationStrategies2025}, a state-of-the-art academic erasure coding implementation optimized for high efficiency.

\paragraph{Implementations}
Our proposed \design is implemented in C++ to ensure high performance, with the codebase comprising approximately 2000 lines of code. Notably, Jerasure and ISA-L are officially supported and integrated into Ceph, enabling direct benchmarking within its ecosystem.
To ensure correctness and fairness in performance measurement, we employ Ceph's built-in benchmarking utility, \texttt{ceph\_erasure\_code\_benchmark}\footnote{\url{https://github.com/ceph/ceph/src/test/erasure-code}}, for comparisons with Jerasure and ISA-L.
% As for comparison with Cerasure, since it is not officially supported by Ceph, we directly compare our \design implementation with its open-source codebase \footnote{\url{https://github.com/WeiWangvv/cerasure}} and follow its original benchmarking methodology to maintain methodological consistency.
For Cerasure (unsupported by Ceph), we benchmark \design against its open-source codebase\footnote{\url{https://github.com/WeiWangvv/cerasure}} following its original methodology to ensure consistency.
Without explicit comments, the experiments are conducted by Ceph's benchmarking utility. The used version of Ceph is v18.2.7.

\paragraph{Evaluation Configurations}
We evaluate all schemes under four representative $(k,m)$ configurations: $(6,2)$, $(8,3)$, $(10,4)$, and $(6,3)$, covering storage overhead ratios from 1.33 to 1.5. These settings are chosen to reflect both prevalent industry practice and prior research: $(6,2)$ is a canonical layout in RAID-6 deployments; $(6,3)$ has been adopted in large-scale cloud storage systems such as Google’s~\cite{fordAvailabilityGloballyDistributed2010}; $(10,4)$ is preferred in certain HDFS-RAID deployments at Facebook~\cite{sathiamoorthyXORingElephantsNovel2013}; and $(8,3)$ matches the evaluation setting used in the original \twotone study~\cite{fuTwotoneShiftXORStorage2021}. For each configuration, the chunk size (i.e., the length of each data or parity chunk) is fixed at 2\,MB. We report the average throughput of repeating 2000 times of encoding and decoding for each experiment.
%The same configurations, chunk size, and iteration counts are applied in both evaluation environments. 

\subsection{Coding Throughput Evaluation}
We first evaluate the overall encoding and decoding performance of \design and the compared baselines in terms of throughput. Note that the experimental results from Ceph’s benchmarking utility and those obtained using Cerasure’s configuration may differ due to differences in setup or implementation. Therefore, we report these results separately.

\noindent \textbf{Encoding Performance.} Table~\ref{tab:ceph-encode} reports the encoding throughput for various $(k,m)$ configurations of \design, ISA-L and Jerasure under Ceph's benchmarking utility. 
% Across all tested configurations, \design achieves substantial gains over both baselines. Compared to the best production-grade solution, ISA-L, throughput improvements range from 29.7\% to 84.1\%, while relative to Jerasure the gains range from 100.0\% to 300.4\%. 
Across all tested configurations, \design achieves substantial gains over both baselines. Compared to the best production-grade solution, ISA-L, throughput improvements range from 29.7\% to 84.1\%, with an average improvement of 59.1\%. Relative to Jerasure, the gains range from 104.0\% to 342.8\%, averaging 261.4\%.
Even in the $(10,4)$ case, where \design's own throughput drops by roughly 50\% relative to its other configurations, it still outperforms both ISA-L and Jerasure. This drop is attributable to the algorithmic structure: for the first three parity blocks, the inter-data shift offsets are 0 or 1 symbol, which can be implemented efficiently and integrated into the neighbor-merging access pattern. The fourth parity block, however, requires a shift offset of 2 symbols, which cannot be merged with the others under AVX2's 256-bit register constraints, thus reducing achievable throughput.

\noindent \textbf{Decoding Performance.} Table~\ref{tab:ceph-decode} presents the decoding throughput across various $(k,m)$ configurations of \design, ISA-L and Jerasure, where the \emph{Erasures} column indicates the number of erased chunks. We highlight the \emph{Erasures} factor as critical, as \design incorporates techniques optimized for distinct erasure cases, particularly for single-chunk loss. For single-chunk erasures, \design achieves remarkable performance gains over ISA-L, with improvements exceeding 130\%. This advantage stems from a direct XOR pass that bypasses the full \ttelim procedure. Overall, \design delivers an average 50.9\% improvement over ISA-L and an average 161.1\% improvement over Jerasure. For ISA-L, the largest gains occur in single-erasure cases, with improvements from 135.4\% to 182.1\%. For Jerasure, the largest gains occur with higher erasure counts (three or four erased chunks), reaching 212.5\% to 328.8\%.

While \design generally outperforms ISA-L, some cases exhibit smaller margins or even slight regressions, most notably in two-chunk erasures and in the $(10,4)$ configuration with more than one chunk erasures.
The decoding slowdown in $(10,4)$ shares the same root cause as observed in encoding: the special 2-symbols shift offset in the fourth parity block disrupts both the \subs and \ttelim phases.
For two-erasure cases, performance is less favorable because \design cannot leverage the direct XOR path used in single-erasure recovery, nor does it benefit from the greater XOR dominance typical of higher-erasure scenarios. Instead, it must execute both \subs and \ttelim in full, leading to reduced efficiency.
Nevertheless, throughput remains close to ISA-L’s, and \design’s encoding performance is consistently higher.

\subsection{End-to-end Performance in File Storage Systems}
% 除了上文基于内存（不涉及I/O）的纠删码吞吐量实验，我们也进行了基于磁盘的端到端吞吐量实验。实验方法是在Ceph中利用vstart功能创建测试集群，在测试集群中创建纠删码存储池，再对存储池进行端到端访问。实验结果如表\ref{tab:put-encode}所示，
In addition to the erasure coding throughput experiments that were conducted in main memory, excluding other CPU operations and disk I/O, we also carried out disk-based end-to-end throughput experiments. Specifically, we utilized the \texttt{vstart}\footnote{\url{https://docs.ceph.com/en/latest/dev/developer_guide/dash-devel/#vstart}} utility in Ceph to set up a test cluster. Within this cluster, we created an \textit{erasure-coded storage pool}\footnote{\url{https://docs.ceph.com/en/latest/rados/operations/erasure-code/}}, and subsequently performed end-to-end \emph{PUT} operations. These operations involves writing a local file as an object into Ceph using the \texttt{rados}\footnote{\url{https://docs.ceph.com/en/latest/man/8/rados/}} utility. It is important to note that the \emph{PUT} operation contains not only the encoding process but also slower disk write operations, resulting in significantly lower speeds compared to the previous experiments. Nevertheless, it remains meaningful to demonstrate the application of \design in a production-grade scenario. The experimental results are presented in Table~\ref{tab:put-encode}. It is observed that \design consistently outperforms ISA-L and yields an average improvement of 7.1\% (up to 13.3\%).

\section{Conclusion}
\label{sec:conclusion}

This paper presents \design, the first high-performance implementation of \twotone, the state-of-the-art Shift–XOR erasure code, tailored for modern storage systems. By addressing key inefficiencies in prior Shift–XOR implementations—such as redundant memory access, suboptimal traversal patterns, and lack of generality, we bridge the gap between theoretical potential and practical deployment. 
Our key innovations comprise four techniques that mitigate the memory-bound bottlenecks for both encoding and decoding, significantly improving the overall coding throughput. 
Validated by extensive experiments with common benchmarking tools,
% \design achieves 36.2–84.1\% higher encoding throughput and 33.5–182.1\% faster decoding compared to state-of-the-art libraries (ISA-L, Jerasure, and Cerasure). 
\design achieves 36.2\% average (up to 84.1\%)  higher encoding throughput and 33.5\% average (up to 182.1\%) faster decoding compared to state-of-the-art libraries (ISA-L and Cerasure). 
End-to-end evaluations in distributed storage settings further demonstrate up to 13.3\% improvement in file storage throughput, confirming its practicality for real-world deployments.

%This paper presents a system-level optimized implementation of \twotone non-cyclic shift–XOR erasure codes, integrated as a production-ready plugin within the Ceph distributed storage system. Motivated by the gap between the theoretical efficiency of shift–XOR codes and their limited practical adoption, we design four complementary system-level optimizations: a generalized decoding framework, a cache-friendly window mechanism with on-demand initialization, a neighbor-merging pattern to reduce memory accesses, and a specialized fast path for single-erasure recovery. These techniques collectively address the unique characteristics of shift–XOR coding that prevent direct reuse of RS-code optimizations.

%Extensive evaluations across diverse $(k,m)$ configurations demonstrate that our implementation achieves up to $150\%$ higher encoding throughput and significantly faster recovery compared to state-of-the-art RS-based implementations such as ISA-L, as well as outperforming existing shift–XOR variants like ZigZag. Beyond raw performance, the integration into Ceph validates the practicality, robustness, and maintainability of the proposed approach in a production-grade environment.

%While this work advances the system-level engineering of shift–XOR erasure codes, further opportunities remain, including adaptive window sizing, more flexible merging heuristics, and exploiting reducible multi-erasure patterns. We believe the methodology and insights developed here provide a solid foundation for future research, and will help unlock the full potential of shift–XOR coding in large-scale, high-performance storage systems.

data.text:
I'm checking the current slide PDF to see what the layout looks like and comparing the figures in the slides directory against what's in the Overleaf project to make sure nothing's missing. I also need to catalog all the paper figures being used in the main document so I can plan which ones to incorporate into the slides. I'm seeing the rest of the supplementary figures available in the figs directory—additional encoding and decoding examples for both two-tone and RS codes, plus special case demonstrations. I should weave these into the slides strategically, particularly the two-tone primer and the approach section, so the audience has visual reinforcement at each step without overwhelming any single slide.

For the structure, I'm thinking the limitations section stays at 2 slides but I'll integrate the encoding example into the first limitations slide and the decoding example into the second, keeping text minimal and letting the figures do the heavy lifting. Then in the approach section, I'll use the access pattern figures and the three key demonstration slides (cache-friendly window, erasure-parity mapping, and vertical traversal) to anchor the technical ideas, with speaker notes carrying the detailed explanation. Idea 2 (slide 8) ✓
- `Erasure-Parity Mapping Decoding Demonstration.pdf` → Key Idea 3 (slide 9) ✓
- `Vertical Traversal Pattern Demonstration.pdf` → Key Idea 4 (slide 10) ✓

So I've placed 8 of the main figures. Let me check if there are other figures I'm missing from the paper that should go in. Looking at what's left to cover: the results slides (11-12) will have tables/charts from the paper's evaluation section. The summary slide (13) might benefit from a visual but isn't critical.

Actually, I should double-check the slide count against the guide limits. With 14 slides total, I'm slightly over the ~13 max, so I might need to consolidate. Could I merge slides 11-12 (both results) into one dense slide? Or combine two of the key idea slides? Let me reconsider whether all four key ideas need separate slides or if some could share space. I'm settling on keeping the encoding and decoding examples in the background slide for context, then using the naive decoding flow figure to highlight the separate repair stage complexity in the limitations section. This gives me a clean structure: motivation, background overview, two limitation slides focusing on access patterns and decoding complexity, then the optimization overview followed by the four key ideas and results. I'm settling on a 14-slide structure with background as a standalone visual bridge, then shifting focus to drastically reducing text density by using short bullets, larger figures, and moving detailed explanations into speaker notes. I'll keep the existing preamble and file structure intact, just rewriting the content with `\small` instead of `\scriptsize` where possible and ensuring the Chinese comments stay internal for my own clarity.

Writing the optimized LaTeX file... Continuing with the key ideas—CFWP tiles chunks into cache-sized windows with on-demand initialization, keeping the window size at least k-1 and a minimal power-of-two. NMA merges adjacent data in SIMD registers and XORs before touching parity, cutting memory complexity roughly in half compared to horizontal traversal. EPM unifies erased data and parity into one decoding flow without a separate repair stage, reducing memory round-trips. TPS optimizes for the most common case of single erasure with a one-pass vertical XOR that bypasses full elimination.

The flagship results show substantial gains: +59.1% average encode and +50.9% average decode versus ISA-L, plus +13.2% and +16.0% improvements over Cerasure across industrial and academic benchmarks. An ablation table breaks down the contribution of each optimization. Wrapping up with a one-sentence summary, three key takeaways, and future directions around adaptive windows, flexible merging, and multi-erasure decomposition.

For the presentation itself, PDPS talks run 15-18 minutes with Q&A, so roughly one minute per slide—speaker notes should land around 120-180 words to fit that pacing. I'm structuring the slides with a consistent two-column layout: motivation with bullets and a visual callout, background showing encoding and decoding examples side by side, then dedicated slides for each limitation and the four key techniques, followed by results and ablation. Writing the LaTeX now...

I'm also adjusting figure aspect ratios since many of the Two-tone diagrams are quite wide; I'll use `keepaspectratio` with a generous height constraint so they don't look cramped in a narrower column. For the section structure, I'm keeping the existing preamble and three-level note template, then reorganizing the content sections: Motivation, Background (with Two-tone at a glance as a subsection), Limitations of Existing Solutions, Approach and Key Ideas, Flagship Results, and Summary & Future Research. This keeps the flow clean while giving each major topic its own section heading that propagates to the notes. Verifying all the figure files are in place, then I'll rewrite the entire LaTeX document while preserving the custom note template preamble and keeping the 16:9 aspect ratio consistent throughout.
